title: MySQL Setup & Configuration
description: Connecting PHP to MySQL databases and understanding storage engines.

sections.title: Connecting PHP to a Database
sections.lesson:
Learning Objectives

  • Understand the role of PHP in database-driven applications.
  • Identify the prerequisites for connecting PHP to MySQL.
  • Learn how to establish a database connection using PHP.
  • Differentiate between MySQLi and PDO.

What is Database Connection in PHP?

Connecting a database to PHP means allowing your PHP program to communicate with a database (such as MySQL) so it can store, retrieve, update, and delete data.

This connection is essential for building dynamic web applications like login systems, online stores, and student information systems.

💡 Example: When a user logs in, PHP checks the username and password stored in the database.

Prerequisites

  • Basic knowledge of PHP and HTML
  • A running web server (e.g., Apache via XAMPP/WAMP)
  • MySQL database installed and running
  • PHP MySQL extension enabled
  • A database with at least one table
⚠️ PHP cannot directly access a database without a server environment.

Required Connection Information

To connect PHP to a MySQL database, you need the following:

  • Server Name (e.g., localhost)
  • Username
  • Password
  • Database Name
💡 These credentials act like a "key" to access your database.

Example: Connecting using MySQLi


<?php
$servername = "localhost";
$username = "root";
$password = "";
$dbname = "my_database";

// Create connection
$conn = mysqli_connect($servername, $username, $password, $dbname);

// Check connection
if (!$conn) {
    die("Connection failed: " . mysqli_connect_error());
}

echo "Connected successfully";
?>

The mysqli_connect() function connects PHP to MySQL using server credentials. If the connection fails, it returns false. :contentReference[oaicite:0]{index=0}

Example: Connecting using PDO


<?php
$host = "localhost";
$dbname = "my_database";
$username = "root";
$password = "";

try {
    $conn = new PDO("mysql:host=$host;dbname=$dbname", $username, $password);
    $conn->setAttribute(PDO::ATTR_ERRMODE, PDO::ERRMODE_EXCEPTION);
    echo "Connected successfully";
} catch(PDOException $e) {
    echo "Connection failed: " . $e->getMessage();
}
?>

PDO (PHP Data Objects) is more flexible because it can work with multiple database systems and supports better error handling. :contentReference[oaicite:1]{index=1}

MySQLi vs PDO

  • MySQLi – works only with MySQL
  • PDO – supports multiple databases
  • Both support prepared statements for security
✅ Best Practice: Use PDO for flexibility and security.

Real-World Example

Imagine a Student Grading System:

  • Students enter their ID
  • PHP connects to the database
  • The system retrieves grades from MySQL
  • Results are displayed on the webpage
💡 Without database connection, dynamic systems like this cannot function.

Basic Flow of Connection

  1. User interacts with a web page
  2. PHP script runs on the server
  3. PHP connects to MySQL
  4. Query is executed
  5. Results are returned and displayed

Reflection Questions

  1. Why is a database connection important in web development?
  2. What happens if the connection fails?
  3. When would you prefer PDO over MySQLi?
  4. What are the risks of incorrect database credentials?

sections.title: MySQL Storage Engines
sections.lesson:
Learning Objectives

  • Define what a storage engine is in a relational database system
  • Explain why different storage engines exist
  • Compare common MySQL storage engines
  • Select an appropriate storage engine for a given use case

What Is a Storage Engine?

A storage engine is the component of a database management system (DBMS) responsible for how data is stored, indexed, retrieved, and managed on disk or in memory.

In MySQL, the SQL layer (queries, joins, functions) is separated from the storage engine layer. This means the same SQL query can work across different storage engines, but performance and features may vary.

Why Are Storage Engines Important?

Different applications have different needs. Some require:

  • High transaction reliability
  • Fast read performance
  • Minimal storage overhead
  • Support for foreign keys and constraints

Storage engines allow database designers to choose how data behaves behind the scenes without changing SQL syntax.

Common MySQL Storage Engines

Storage Engine Description Key Features
InnoDB Default and most widely used storage engine Transactions, foreign keys, row-level locking, crash recovery
MyISAM Older engine focused on fast read operations Table-level locking, no transactions, faster reads
MEMORY Stores data in RAM instead of disk Extremely fast access, data lost on restart
CSV Stores data as CSV text files Simple format, easy data exchange, limited performance

InnoDB vs MyISAM (Quick Comparison)

Feature InnoDB MyISAM
Transactions ✔ Supported ✖ Not Supported
Foreign Keys ✔ Supported ✖ Not Supported
Locking Row-level Table-level
Crash Recovery ✔ Yes ✖ No

Specifying a Storage Engine

A storage engine can be specified when creating a table. 


CREATE TABLE students (
    student_id INT PRIMARY KEY,
    name VARCHAR(100),
    course VARCHAR(50)
) ENGINE = InnoDB;

If no engine is specified, MySQL uses the default storage engine (usually InnoDB).

When to Use Which Engine?

  • InnoDB – Best for most applications, especially transactional systems
  • MyISAM – Suitable for read-heavy, non-transactional data
  • MEMORY – Temporary tables or fast lookups
  • CSV – Simple data exchange or reporting

Key Takeaways

  • Storage engines control how data is stored and managed
  • Different engines offer different features and performance
  • InnoDB is the modern default and recommended engine
  • Choosing the right engine improves reliability and efficiency
